subject: ap-psychology
theme: Social psychology examines how people's thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are influenced by others, while personality theories — psychoanalytic, humanistic, trait, and social-cognitive — explain stable individual differences in behavior.

core_concepts.title: Attribution Theory & Biases
core_concepts.detail: Attribution theory: the fundamental attribution error (FAE) is the tendency to overattribute others' behavior to dispositional causes and underestimate situational factors. The actor-observer bias contrasts how we explain our own (situational) vs. others' (dispositional) behavior. The self-serving bias attributes our successes to internal causes and failures to external causes, protecting self-esteem.

core_concepts.title: Attitude Change & Cognitive Dissonance
core_concepts.detail: Attitude change: Petty and Cacioppo's elaboration likelihood model (ELM) identifies two routes — central route (careful argument evaluation → durable attitude change) and peripheral route (superficial cues: attractiveness, authority, social proof → temporary change). Cognitive dissonance (Festinger) drives attitude change when behavior and beliefs conflict — people reduce dissonance by changing attitudes, behaviors, or adding new cognitions.

core_concepts.title: Conformity, Compliance & Obedience
core_concepts.detail: Social influence: conformity (Asch) increases with unanimous majority and ambiguity; one ally breaks conformity dramatically. Compliance techniques: foot-in-the-door (small commitment first increases later compliance), door-in-the-face (large then smaller request). Obedience (Milgram) is increased by legitimate authority, physical proximity of authority, gradual escalation, and diffused responsibility.

core_concepts.title: Group Processes & Bystander Effect
core_concepts.detail: Group processes: social facilitation — the presence of others increases dominant response strength (improves simple tasks, impairs complex ones). Social loafing — reduced individual effort in group tasks. Groupthink — cohesive groups suppress dissent, leading to poor decisions. Group polarization — discussion amplifies the initially dominant attitude. Bystander effect — helping decreases as group size increases due to diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance.

core_concepts.title: Prejudice & In-Group Bias
core_concepts.detail: Prejudice, discrimination, and in-group bias: social identity theory (Tajfel) explains in-group favoritism as a means of boosting self-esteem. Stereotype threat (Steele) — awareness of a negative group stereotype impairs performance by increasing anxiety and cognitive load.

core_concepts.title: Psychoanalytic, Humanistic & Trait Theories
core_concepts.detail: Personality theories: Freud's psychoanalytic model — id (pleasure principle), ego (reality principle), superego (internalized morality); defense mechanisms (repression, projection, rationalization, displacement). Rogers's humanistic theory — self-concept, ideal self, unconditional positive regard, and conditions of worth. Big Five trait model (OCEAN): Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism — stable, cross-culturally replicable dimensions with moderate heritability.

core_concepts.title: Social-Cognitive Theory & Personality Assessment
core_concepts.detail: Bandura's social-cognitive theory: reciprocal determinism — person (beliefs, self-efficacy), behavior, and environment mutually influence each other. Self-efficacy — belief in one's ability to succeed — is the strongest predictor of goal-directed behavior. Personality assessment: objective tests (MMPI — standardized, high reliability/validity) vs. projective tests (Rorschach, TAT — lower reliability/validity, assess unconscious material).

exam_tip: FRQ scenario prompts frequently require applying a specific social influence concept (FAE, cognitive dissonance, bystander effect) — state the concept by name and clearly connect it to the behavior described in the scenario.
